title: MoE Models (Mixture of Experts)
lesson:
A Mixture of Experts (MoE) model is a clever trick: build a model that is huge in total, but does only a small amount of work for each word it reads.

To see why that matters, picture what it improves on — the dense model. There, every parameter (a learned number inside the model) is used for every single token (roughly, a word). Big and powerful — but every prediction pays the full bill.

The hospital analogy

Imagine a hospital with one brilliant doctor who personally handles every patient — broken arms, heart problems, rashes, everything. To improve, that doctor must cram more and more into one head. That's a dense model: one set of weights doing all the work.

Now imagine a hospital with 32 specialist doctors and a friendly receptionist out front. A patient walks in; the receptionist takes one look and says "that's a heart thing — go to doctor 7 and doctor 19." Only those two are bothered; the other 30 stay idle for this patient.

The hospital contains vast expertise, but any one patient uses only a sliver. That is exactly how MoE works.

The two pieces: experts and a router

An MoE layer replaces the single big block of a dense model with many smaller blocks called experts — each just its own little neural network. In front of them sits the router (or gating network): a tiny network that reads each token and picks which experts handle it.

  • The router picks only a few. A model might hold 64 experts but route each token to just 2.
  • The chosen ones are active; the rest sit idle for that token.

This gives MoE its defining split between two parameter counts:

Total parametersActive parameters
Every weight in the model — all experts added up. Sets how much the model can know and how much memory it needs.The weights actually used per token — router plus the few chosen experts. Sets how fast and cheap each token is.

So a model billed as "235B total, 22B active" stores 235 billion parameters but fires only ~22 billion per token. You get the knowledge of a giant at roughly the compute cost of a small model. That's the whole pitch.

A second analogy: a big consulting firm

Picture a consulting firm with hundreds of staff covering law, tax, design, and engineering. When a client emails, a project manager skims the request and pulls in just two relevant specialists — not the whole firm.

The payroll (total parameters) is enormous; the cost of any one job (active parameters) is small. MoE buys breadth without paying for it on every token.

What it costs you

There is no free lunch. The catch is VRAM — the fast memory on a GPU. Even though only 2 of 64 experts run per token, all 64 must be loaded into memory, because you never know in advance which the router will pick.

⚠️ MoE saves you compute (the maths per token, which sets speed) but not memory. A 235B MoE needs the VRAM of a 235B model even though it runs at the speed of a ~22B one.

You won't usually write router code by hand, but the loading concept is simple to picture:

# Toy MoE layer: pick the top-2 experts for one token
scores = router(token)          # one score per expert, e.g. 64 numbers
top = scores.topk(2)            # choose the 2 highest-scoring experts
out = 0
for i, weight in zip(top.indices, top.values.softmax(0)):
    out += weight * experts[i](token)   # only these 2 actually compute
# the other 62 experts sit idle for this token

Where you meet MoE in 2026

Most frontier open-weight models now ship as MoE — families like Mixtral, DeepSeek, Qwen, and Llama's larger releases use it, and several closed models are widely believed to as well.

The reason is plain economics. For inference (answering prompts at scale), MoE lets a provider serve enormous, smart models without paying full compute on every token — which keeps the cost per token down. When you read a model card boasting a giant total size but a modest active size, you're looking at a Mixture of Experts — and now you know exactly what that trade means.
mentalModel: A hospital with 32 specialists and one receptionist out front. Every patient (token) gets seen by the receptionist (the router), who sends them to just 1-2 relevant specialists. The building holds enormous expertise (total parameters), but any single patient only uses a sliver of it (active parameters). You pay rent on the whole hospital (VRAM) but only the wages of the doctors you actually use (compute).
takeaways: MoE = many small experts plus a router; only a few fire per token.
Total params set knowledge and VRAM; active params set speed and cost.
Saves compute, not memory — every expert still loads into VRAM.

diagram.svg: <svg class="dg" viewBox="0 0 720 340" role="img" aria-label="A router sends one token to 2 of 4 experts in an MoE layer"><defs><marker id="arrow" markerWidth="12" markerHeight="12" refX="9" refY="5" orient="auto"><path class="dg-arrowhead" d="M0,0 L11,5 L0,10 z"/></marker></defs><text class="dg-title" x="360" y="34" text-anchor="middle">One token, only 2 of 4 experts fire</text><rect class="dg-box" x="40" y="140" width="120" height="60" rx="12"/><text class="dg-label" x="100" y="175" text-anchor="middle">Token</text><line class="dg-edge" x1="160" y1="170" x2="230" y2="170" marker-end="url(#arrow)"/><rect class="dg-box dg-box-accent" x="230" y="135" width="130" height="70" rx="12"/><text class="dg-label" x="295" y="165" text-anchor="middle">Router</text><text class="dg-sub" x="295" y="186" text-anchor="middle">picks top-2</text><line class="dg-edge" x1="360" y1="150" x2="500" y2="75" marker-end="url(#arrow)"/><line class="dg-edge" x1="360" y1="185" x2="500" y2="235" marker-end="url(#arrow)"/><rect class="dg-box dg-box-good" x="500" y="50" width="170" height="56" rx="12"/><text class="dg-label" x="585" y="83" text-anchor="middle">Expert 1 (active)</text><rect class="dg-box" x="500" y="114" width="170" height="48" rx="12"/><text class="dg-sub" x="585" y="143" text-anchor="middle">Expert 2 — idle</text><rect class="dg-box dg-box-good" x="500" y="210" width="170" height="56" rx="12"/><text class="dg-label" x="585" y="243" text-anchor="middle">Expert 3 (active)</text><rect class="dg-box" x="500" y="274" width="170" height="48" rx="12"/><text class="dg-sub" x="585" y="303" text-anchor="middle">Expert 4 — idle</text><text class="dg-note" x="100" y="250" text-anchor="middle">all 4 loaded in VRAM</text></svg>
diagram.caption: The router reads the token and activates only 2 of the 4 experts, yet all 4 must stay loaded in memory.

mistakes: Thinking MoE saves memory. It saves compute, not VRAM. Every expert must be loaded even though only a couple run per token, because the router's choice is unknown ahead of time. A '235B total, 22B active' model still needs ~235B worth of memory to hold.

Comparing models only by total parameters. A 235B MoE and a 235B dense model are not the same beast: the MoE runs far faster and cheaper because only its active parameters compute. Compare active size for speed and cost, total size for knowledge and memory.

Assuming each expert is a tidy specialist. The 'heart doctor, tax lawyer' picture is an analogy, not reality. Experts learn whatever the router finds useful during training; their specialisms are statistical and rarely map to human categories like 'maths' or 'French'. Don't expect to point at expert 12 and call it the coding one.

Forgetting the router can become a bottleneck. If the router lazily sends most tokens to the same few experts, the rest barely train and the model wastes its capacity. Real MoE training adds a 'load-balancing' nudge to spread tokens evenly across experts — without it, the mixture quietly collapses into a small dense model.

Expecting MoE to be strictly better than dense. For a fixed memory budget, a dense model often gives stronger answers per gigabyte, because all its weights actually compute. MoE wins when you have memory to spare and want speed and breadth; it is a trade, not a free upgrade.

Mis-quantizing MoE on a small machine. Because so many experts sit unused per token, naive 4-bit quantization can hurt MoE quality more than people expect, and the still-large memory footprint surprises beginners who only saw the small 'active' number. Check the real GGUF file size, not the active count, before assuming it fits.
exercise:
Go to a model hub like Hugging Face and find two well-known MoE models (for example a Mixtral and a Qwen or DeepSeek MoE). For each, write down: the total parameter count, the active parameter count, the number of experts, and how many experts are active per token (often written as 'top-k'). Then, for one of them, estimate the VRAM two ways using parameters × bytes-per-parameter: once with the total count and once with the active count. Notice which number governs 'does it fit on my GPU?' and which governs 'how fast will it run?'

Stretch goal: Pull a quantized GGUF version of one MoE model and run it locally with Ollama or llama.cpp. Watch your memory fill up as the model loads (it should track the total size), then time a few responses and compare the speed to a dense model of similar total size. Write one sentence explaining what you observed about memory versus speed.
solution: You'll find totals and active sizes like 'Mixtral 8x7B: ~47B total, ~13B active, 8 experts, top-2' or a DeepSeek/Qwen MoE in the hundreds of billions total with only ~20-37B active. The key realisation: the total count drives VRAM, so that's the number that decides whether it loads at all — a 47B-total model in 16-bit needs roughly 47e9 × 2 = 94 GB, far more than the 13B-active figure suggests (~26 GB). The active count drives speed and cost, so the model runs about as fast as a 13B dense model despite holding 47B of knowledge. For the stretch goal you should see memory usage climb to roughly the full quantized file size (all experts loaded), yet generation speed feel close to a much smaller dense model — memory tracks total, speed tracks active. That gap, in one sentence, is the entire reason MoE exists.
summary: A Mixture of Experts model splits each layer into many small 'expert' networks plus a tiny 'router' that sends every token to just a few of them. This separates two numbers: total parameters (all experts, which set knowledge and memory needs) and active parameters (the few used per token, which set speed and cost). MoE buys you the smarts of a giant model at the compute price of a small one — but you still pay full VRAM, because every expert must be loaded even though most stay idle. Compare active size for speed, total size for memory.

keyTerms.def: A model design where each layer holds many small sub-networks ('experts'), and only a few run for each token. Gives huge total capacity at a small per-token compute cost.
One of the many small neural networks inside an MoE layer. For any given token, only a couple of experts are chosen to run; the rest sit idle.
A tiny network in front of the experts that reads each token and decides which experts should handle it. Like a receptionist directing patients to the right specialists.
The count of every weight in the model, all experts added together. Determines how much the model can know and how much memory (VRAM) it needs to load.
The weights actually used to process a single token — the router plus the chosen experts. Determines how fast and cheap each token is to compute.
The rule for how many experts handle each token, e.g. 'top-2' means the router picks the 2 highest-scoring experts out of all of them for that token.
A training nudge that spreads tokens evenly across experts. Without it, the router over-uses a few experts, the rest never learn, and the model wastes its capacity.
The idea that only a small fraction of the model's weights 'fire' for any given token. This sparsity is what lets MoE be enormous in total yet cheap per token.

quiz.q: A model card says '120B total, 14B active'. What does the 14B mainly tell you?
options: How much VRAM you need to load it
How fast and cheap each token is to generate
How many experts the model contains
The file size of the quantized download
quiz.explain: Active parameters are the weights actually computed per token, so they govern speed and cost. VRAM and download size are driven by the total (120B), not the active count.

quiz.q: You only have a 24 GB GPU. Why might a '47B total, 13B active' MoE still fail to load?
options: The router needs its own dedicated GPU
Only the 13B active part is stored on disk
All experts must be in VRAM, so memory tracks the 47B total
quiz.explain: The router can pick any expert per token, so every expert must be resident in memory. Memory tracks total size (47B), which far exceeds the 13B-active hint.

quiz.q: During training, the router keeps sending most tokens to the same 3 experts. What's the likely consequence?
options: The model trains faster with no downside
The other experts barely learn, wasting the model's capacity
VRAM usage drops because idle experts are deleted
The active parameter count automatically increases
quiz.explain: Unbalanced routing starves most experts of training signal, so the mixture collapses toward a small dense model. This is why MoE training adds a load-balancing nudge.

quiz.q: For a fixed memory budget, when is a dense model often the better pick over an MoE?
options: When you want the most answer quality per gigabyte loaded
When you need the fastest possible tokens per second
When you want the largest possible total knowledge
When you are serving millions of requests cheaply
quiz.explain: Every dense weight computes, so per gigabyte of VRAM it often gives stronger answers. MoE wins on speed and breadth when you have spare memory, but it is a trade-off, not a free upgrade.
